blueprint_refs: Blueprint metric: "category_mix_jsd" defined on fine categories (or super-categories optionally).
Blueprint: "Extract Category from tokens; optionally map to super-category via catto.json"

evidence.metrics: category_share_mae=0.0245 looks very low while topk_poi_recall=0.087 is low; evaluator uses super-category shares only (sc_of_visit), not fine-category mix.

missing-fine-category-metrics-computed-a.error_identification: Reported category distribution error is very low, but it is computed over super-categories only, not the blueprint’s fine-category distribution, making the metric less discriminative and potentially masking category-level mismatches.
missing-fine-category-metrics-computed-a.root_cause_analysis: In Evaluator.compute_metrics(), category counts are built via sc_of_visit() which maps each visit to super-category (catto.json) and then computes category_share_mae and category_mix_jsd on those super-categories. This diverges from the blueprint’s required metric definition which is fine-category by default (super-category is optional, not the only mode).
missing-fine-category-metrics-computed-a.correct_approach: Add a fine-category path in Evaluator.compute_metrics(): extract fine category from token (split on '#'), compute fine-category counts and JSD/MAE on that distribution. Keep existing super-category metrics if desired, but ensure the primary reported metric matches blueprint intent (and name).
missing-fine-category-metrics-computed-a.key_insight: If the blueprint allows an optional aggregation (super-category), implement both and clearly report which one drives selection; otherwise you risk optimizing an easier proxy metric that hides failures.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Online OOD context window rule: ... construct a 7-day context window ..."
Blueprint: POI choice should blend personal preference and distance decay.
poi-recall-low-due-to-no-personal-poi-me.error_identification: POI overlap with ground truth is very low (recall@5 ~ 0.09), indicating the simulator rarely hits the user’s true POIs on a given day.
poi-recall-low-due-to-no-personal-poi-me.root_cause_analysis: The context mechanism only uses the last POI from prior days as a starting point (_context_last_poi), but does not condition the day’s POI candidates on the 7-day context window beyond that. In Resident.simulate_day(), candidates are mostly (a) top historical POIs from 2019–2020 by supercat and (b) global popular POIs; this can miss 2021-specific recurring POIs that appear in recent 2021 context (e.g., new home/work or changed routines), which the blueprint explicitly allows using earlier 2021 days in the context window.
poi-recall-low-due-to-no-personal-poi-me.correct_approach: Extend _context_last_poi into a context feature extractor that returns recent POIs and/or per-supercat POI counts from the preceding 7 days (strictly earlier than target date). In Resident.simulate_day(), inject these context POIs into the candidate set with a strong prior (e.g., additional additive weight or separate mixture component). Ensure strict no-leakage (exclude target day).
poi-recall-low-due-to-no-personal-poi-me.key_insight: A context window should influence more than the initial state; use it to bias the full choice set toward recently seen user-specific POIs to improve recall under regime shifts.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: baseline_stop_count_dist (per-user) and abnormal shift via theta_stop_count_multiplier
Blueprint: daily plan uses start_time, stop_count, and inter-event gaps with feasibility constraints
stop-count-error-high-missing-daylength-.error_identification: The simulator’s per-day number of visits is off by ~2 on average, despite matching the average visits/day reasonably closely in diagnostics.
stop-count-error-high-missing-daylength-.root_cause_analysis: Stop count is sampled from a tilted baseline PMF, but there is no coupling between sampled stop_count and feasible time budget beyond a minimal `end_min - (stop_count-1)` guard. Because gaps are sampled from an empirical gap list without conditioning on stop_count or time-of-day, the model can produce unrealistic dense late-day chains or compress chains to fit, which destabilizes effective stop realization vs the intended distribution and yields high MAE.
stop-count-error-high-missing-daylength-.correct_approach: Condition gap sampling on remaining time budget: when stop_count is high, sample smaller gaps (or rescale sampled gaps) to maintain feasibility without truncation artifacts; when stop_count is low, allow larger gaps. Alternatively, model an explicit end time distribution and sample stop_count consistent with (end-start)/expected_gap. Keep the existing PMF tilt but add a second-stage feasibility-consistent adjustment rather than silent clipping.
stop-count-error-high-missing-daylength-.key_insight: Stop count, gaps, and day time budget must be jointly consistent; sampling them independently and then clipping times tends to inflate count errors and distort temporal statistics.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: time_of_day_emd on minute-of-day distribution; simulator can operate in minute granularity with HH:MM:SS output
time-distribution-mismatch-from-coarse-w.error_identification: Time-of-day distribution mismatch remains moderate (JSD ~ 0.105), indicating simulated visit times are shifted/spread differently than ground truth.
time-distribution-mismatch-from-coarse-w.root_cause_analysis: Start times are sampled from a binned histogram (15-minute bins) and then uniformly within-bin, then shifted by theta_start_time_shift_minutes. Subsequent times are driven by gap sampling that is not conditioned on time-of-day; additionally, the code retries start time up to 8 times to force an “active window” (04:00–23:30), which can reshape the distribution in a way not present in the baseline data.
time-distribution-mismatch-from-coarse-w.correct_approach: Fit and sample start times at minute resolution (or use 5-minute bins) without the hard active-window rejection loop; if you need constraints, incorporate them into the learned baseline (e.g., truncated distribution) so sampling matches data. Condition gaps on time-of-day bins or remaining minutes to avoid piling events near midnight.
time-distribution-mismatch-from-coarse-w.key_insight: Avoid post-hoc rejection rules on learned temporal distributions; encode constraints directly into the fitted distribution or you will create distribution shifts that show up as TOD divergence.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: radius_of_gyration_error requires POI lat/lon join; handle missing POIs by excluding those visits

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.9993 with diagnostics showing gt_missing_step_fraction≈0.70 and sim_missing_step_fraction≈0.47.

spatial-metric-degradation-from-high-gt-.error_identification: Trip distance mismatch is sizable, and evaluation indicates extremely high missing coordinate rates for ground-truth steps (~70%), making distance-based metrics noisy and biased.
spatial-metric-degradation-from-high-gt-.root_cause_analysis: Despite added normalization/casefold indices, many ground-truth POI tokens still fail to resolve to POI catalog entries, so step distances are missing and dropped. This leads to an apples-to-oranges comparison: simulated steps have higher coordinate availability than GT, so the two distance distributions are computed on different subsets.
spatial-metric-degradation-from-high-gt-.correct_approach: Improve token canonicalization beyond whitespace/punctuation: implement a strictly verified mapping only when the exact token exists in the catalog; log the top unresolved fine categories/tokens to diagnose systematic mismatches. Additionally, when computing trip_distance_wasserstein, enforce the same missingness policy for sim and GT (e.g., only compare steps where both endpoints resolve in both sim and GT, or downsample sim steps to match GT resolvability) to reduce bias.
spatial-metric-degradation-from-high-gt-.key_insight: If a metric depends on external joins, missingness can dominate; measure and equalize join success across simulated and GT before trusting distribution distances.

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.9993 and topk_poi_recall=0.0873; code clamps w_pref to <=0.9.

poi-choice-mixture-capped-too-high-limit.error_identification: The calibrated preference-vs-distance mixture cannot reach the blueprint’s full [0,1] range, which can limit calibration’s ability to trade off personalization vs spatial plausibility.
poi-choice-mixture-capped-too-high-limit.root_cause_analysis: Resident.simulate_day() clamps w_pref with `min(0.9, w_pref)` even though the blueprint allows up to 1.0. This prevents “pure preference” regimes that may be needed to improve POI recall for users with strong habitual POIs, especially when category modeling is coarse.
poi-choice-mixture-capped-too-high-limit.correct_approach: Clamp theta_preference_vs_distance_mixture to [0.0, 1.0] exactly as specified. If numerical issues motivated the cap, address them by adding a small floor to distance weights or preference weights (already done) rather than changing the parameter domain.
poi-choice-mixture-capped-too-high-limit.key_insight: Don’t silently change calibrated parameter bounds; calibration depends on the search space matching the spec.
